FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, two-year follow-up data from 1,036 participants aged ~12 years from 21 US sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic gaming severity is the outcome variable; no qualifying smartphone or social media exposure variable. Connectome-based predictive modeling of problematic gaming (Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale score) predicts FROM neural connectivity, not from SM/smartphone exposure. Applies B5 (unspecified gaming without separate SM coefficient).

FT_IC3.evidence: Connectome-based predictive modelling (CPM) with 10-fold cross-validation, correlation between predicted and observed VGAQ scores (r = 0.12, p = 0.002), permutation testing with 1,000 iterations

FT_IC4.evidence: r = 0.12, p = 0.002; edge weights reported for predictive networks

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis using connectome-based predictive modeling of problematic gaming

FT_IC6.evidence: Full-length report published 2026, no indication of preprint/abstract/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Criterion not applicable. Gaming severity is the dependent variable, but no qualifying smartphone/SM exposure is an independent variable (B5 - unspecified gaming alone).
notes: FT_IC2 fails: paper examines neural predictors of problematic gaming (outcome), not SM/smartphone predictors. Gaming without separate SM or app-category breakdown does not meet qualifying exposure definition.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, 5.0 release, nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10 years from 21 US research sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (total and by digital device: television, videos, video games, texting, video chat) as independent variable. Meets B1 (total screen time reported; also examined by device). Applies B1 rule.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models with each cognitive domain as dependent variable; mediation analysis via bootstrap with 10,000 resamples; multiple t-tests for indirect/total effects

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients reported (e.g., executive function b = -0.212, 95% CI = -0.301 to -0.122, p < .001; indirect effects β = -0.015, 95% CI = -0.028 to -0.005, p = .016 for mediation); FDR-adjusted p-values in main text and tables

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data examining association between screen time and cognitive function with putamen volume as mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2026 in Acta Psychologica, full-length article, no indication of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Screen time is the independent variable (IV), not dependent variable. Paper examines screen time → cognitive function with putamen as mediator (B18 carve-out: has SM-as-IV → outcome analysis, so FT-EC7 does not apply).
notes: All FT-IC criteria met. Cross-sectional design limits causal inference but meets screening criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, two-year longitudinal cohort (T1 and T2), 1,186 participants aged 9-10 years at baseline, recruited from 21 US sites with school-based probability sampling

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the independent/predictor variable, not a qualifying smartphone or social media exposure. Cyberbullying alone without separately measured SM with own coefficient does not meet criteria (B3). Social media use mentioned in covariates/table but paper investigates cyberbullying victimization → mental health/neural outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects models (LMMs) with longitudinal associations; t-tests for cyberbullying at T1 predicting outcomes at T2; mediation analysis via bootstrap with 5,000 resamples and inverse probability weighting

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients and 95% CIs reported (e.g., cyberbullying → externalizing problems β = 0.25, 95% CI 0.06-0.45, p = .01; brain activation contrasts β = 0.36, 95% CI 0.10-0.61, p = .01); F-statistics, p-values with FDR adjustment

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal neuroimaging analysis of ABCD data examining cyberbullying victimization associations with inhibitory control, neural activation, and mental health

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2026 in Journal of Medical Internet Research, full-length original paper, no indication of preprint/abstract/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Criterion not applicable. Cyberbullying victimization (not a qualifying SM/smartphone exposure) is the independent variable; FT_IC2 fails.
notes: FT_IC2 fails: cyberbullying alone without separately measured SM coefficient does not qualify as a qualifying smartphone or social media exposure variable (B3). While the paper is longitudinal and uses ABCD data, the exposure of interest is not SM/smartphone.
